stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \) using the known series \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n x^{n} = \dfrac{x}{(1-x)^{2}} \) for \( |x|<1 \).
distractor_rationales: This is the plain geometric sum \( \frac{x}{1-x} \), dropping the factor of \( n \).
This uses \( \frac{1}{(1-x)^{2}} \), forgetting the \( x \) in the numerator.
This uses \( (1+x)^{2} \) in the denominator instead of \( (1-x)^{2} \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.

stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \) using the known geometric series \( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-x} \) for \( |x|<1 \).
distractor_rationales: This is the arithmetico-geometric sum \( \sum n x^{n} = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2} \), the wrong known series.
This uses \( 1+x \) in the denominator instead of \( 1-x \).
This inverts the ratio \( x \) before applying the formula.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.

stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^{2}\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \) using the known series \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^{2} x^{n} = \dfrac{x(1+x)}{(1-x)^{3}} \) for \( |x|<1 \).
distractor_rationales: This is the plain arithmetico-geometric sum \( \sum n x^n = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2} \), missing the \( n^2 \) formula's extra factor and power.
This uses the wrong power \( (1-x)^2 \) instead of \( (1-x)^3 \) in the denominator.
This drops the leading \( x \) factor in the numerator.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.

stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[c]]^{n}}{n!} \) using the known Maclaurin series \( e^{x} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{x^{n}}{n!} \).
solution: This is \( e^{x} \) evaluated at \( x=[[c]] \): \( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{[[c]]^{n}}{n!} = e^{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( -c \) in the exponent instead of \( c \).
This multiplies \( c \) by \( e \) instead of using \( c \) as the exponent.
This is only the first two terms of the series (\( n=0,1 \)), not the full sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.

stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[c]]^{n}}{n!} \) using the known Maclaurin series \( e^{x} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{x^{n}}{n!} \). (Note this sum starts at \( n=1 \), not \( n=0 \).)
solution: \( \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{[[c]]^{n}}{n!} = e^{[[c]]} \); removing the \( n=0 \) term (which equals \( 1 \)) leaves \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{[[c]]^{n}}{n!} = e^{[[c]]} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to remove the \( n=0 \) term from the full series.
This adds the removed term back instead of subtracting it.
This uses \( -c \) in the exponent and still subtracts \( 1 \) incorrectly.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.

stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)!}\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{2n+1} \) using the known Maclaurin series \( \sin(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{n}x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!} \).
solution: This is \( \sin(x) \) evaluated at \( x=\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \): the sum equals \( \sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the cosine series instead of the sine series.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This is just the argument \( x \) itself (the first-order Taylor approximation), not the true sum.
This inverts the argument before applying sine.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.

stem: Evaluate the series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{n}}{2n+1}\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{2n+1} \) using the known Maclaurin series \( \arctan(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{n}x^{2n+1}}{2n+1} \) for \( |x|\le 1 \).
solution: This is \( \arctan(x) \) evaluated at \( x=\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \): the sum equals \( \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the argument \( x \) itself (the first-order Taylor approximation), not the true sum.
This has the wrong overall sign.
This inverts the argument before applying arctan.
This is the special value \( \arctan(1) \), regardless of the actual argument here.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This doubles the correct sum.
